name: Central African Republic
experiences: Bangui's cathedral quarter, Boganda National Museum, and practical riverfront base for arranging vetted transport
Boali Falls and the hydro gorge on a dry-season day trip where a 4x4 is the sensible choice
Dzanga Bai forest-elephant viewing from the raised platform in one of Central Africa's signature wildlife sites
Western lowland gorilla tracking in Dzanga-Sangha with mandatory official guides and porters
Pirogue rides on the Sangha and Ba'Aka cultural encounters around Bayanga when arranged through trusted lodges
avoid: Strong safety advisory: the Central African Republic has an active conflict environment and many governments advise against travel; only consider essential, professionally arranged travel, keep plans flexible, and do not move independently after dark.
Road travel beyond Bangui is slow and security-sensitive, so treat lodge-arranged 4x4 transfers or charter flights as the default rather than improvising buses, bush taxis, or self-drive plans.
ATMs, medical support, and mobile data are limited outside Bangui; carry enough cash, maintain evacuation-capable insurance, and expect weak signal in Bayanga and on forest legs.
stopoverNote: Bangui is the only realistic logistics base: sleep there before and after Dzanga-Sangha, lock in a vetted fixer or lodge first, and treat Boali as a dry-season daylight outing rather than squeezing everything into tight same-day onward transfers.

cities.name: Bangui
cities.notes: Bangui Cathedral, Boganda National Museum, secure hotel compounds, cash/SIM logistics, and the only practical jumping-off point for Boali or Dzanga-Sangha.

cities.name: Dzanga-Sangha Reserve
cities.notes: Bayanga-based rainforest access for Dzanga Bai, western lowland gorilla tracking, Sangha pirogues, and Ba'Aka cultural visits, all arranged through lodges or operators.

cities.name: Boali Falls
cities.notes: Classic Bangui day trip for the waterfall viewpoint and hydro station, with limited lodging on site and much better accommodation back in the capital.

itinerary.sim: Orange Centrafrique and Telecel Centrafrique are the practical Bangui options; expect roughly ₹700–₹1,700 for a starter top-up, then plan on patchy or near-nonexistent data once you leave the capital for Boali or Bayanga.

days.theme: Cathedral quarter, museum stop, and settling into the capital's safest hotel zones before any onward travel

activities.name: Hotel-arranged airport transfer into the city
Bangui Cathedral donation at Cathédrale Notre-Dame de l'Immaculée Conception
Boganda National Museum entry
Trusted driver or taxi hops between Centre-Ville, the museum, and riverfront hotels

days.theme: Buffer day for permits, cash, and riverfront orientation before the harder road or charter legs

activities.name: Driver-assisted stop for cash, provisions, and permit reconfirmation
Oubangui riverfront stroll or short local pirogue ride if conditions are calm
Hotel-arranged security briefing or charter/4x4 coordination for Dzanga-Sangha
Short city taxi hops between hotel compounds and the river

days.theme: Dry-season waterfall day trip from Bangui with a 4x4, viewpoint stops, and no pressure to overnight locally

activities.name: Private 4x4 from Bangui to Boali and back
Boali Falls site entry or local access fee
Hydroelectric dam viewpoint or local guide contribution
Lunch stop or coffee break near the falls

days.theme: Reach Bayanga by lodge-arranged charter or expedition 4x4, then head out to the bai for forest-elephant viewing

activities.name: Bangui to Bayanga charter seat or operator-arranged transfer
Dzanga-Sangha reserve entry fee
Dzanga Bai forest-elephant viewing session
Sangha River pirogue transfer or sunset ride

days.theme: Western lowland gorilla tracking, Ba'Aka culture, and another night inside the forest corridor

activities.name: Western lowland gorilla tracking permit
Official tracker or porter tip pool
Ba'Aka community visit or cultural contribution
Short pirogue or 4x4 hop between Bayanga, the river, and trailheads

connections.method: A hired 4x4 with a vetted driver is the only sensible daylight day-trip format; road quality and roadside checks make improvised transport a poor idea.
Return to Bangui first, then continue by lodge-arranged charter or a fully organized multi-day 4x4 transfer; there is no dependable independent connection worth planning around.
Use the same operator structure back out: limited charters are fastest, while overland 4x4 returns are long and should only run with current local security clearance.

extras: December to March is the workable dry-season window: forest trails are easier, road washouts are less common, and Boali and Bayanga logistics are more predictable than in the long rains.

Treat the country as an active-conflict destination, not a casual add-on. If you do go, use hotel or lodge transfers only, keep a daylight-only movement rule, and leave schedule slack for cancellations or security holds.

Dzanga-Sangha is best booked as a package because permits, guides, river transfers, and charter coordination are tightly linked; trying to split-book each piece independently is usually false economy.
Carry yellow-fever proof, malaria prophylaxis, backup cash, and a power bank; Bangui can manage basics, but Bayanga and road stops do not reliably support last-minute medical or payment needs.
Domestic flights are limited and often effectively charter-only, so always leave a Bangui buffer night before any international departure rather than trusting a same-day connection from the forest.
